examQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes a mucous membrane?
options: Lines cavities not open to the exterior; has parietal and visceral layers
Lines passageways with external connections; has a lamina propria of areolar tissue
Covers the surface of the body; is thick, dry, and waterproof
Lines articulating joint cavities; lacks true epithelium
examQuestions.explanation: Mucous membranes (mucosae) line passageways that communicate with the exterior (digestive, respiratory, urinary, reproductive tracts). Their surfaces must stay moist. The underlying connective tissue is the lamina propria (areolar tissue). Choice A describes serous membranes; C describes cutaneous membrane; D describes synovial membranes.

examQuestions.question: A spinal cord section shows large cells with multiple branching processes extending from a central body containing a prominent nucleus. These cells transmit electrical signals. Which cell type is shown?
examQuestions.explanation: Neurons have a cell body (soma) with a prominent nucleus, dendrites (multiple branching processes that receive input), and an axon (conducts output to other cells). Neuroglia are smaller supporting cells. The description of multiple branching processes from a central body containing a nucleus is classic neuron morphology.

examQuestions.question: Which connective tissue is classified as a fluid connective tissue and functions primarily for transportation?
examQuestions.explanation: Blood is a fluid connective tissue whose ground substance is plasma. It transports cells (erythrocytes, leukocytes, platelets) and dissolved substances. Reticular, areolar, and adipose are connective tissue proper.

examQuestions.question: A patient has a myopathy affecting fusiform cells with a single central nucleus and no striations in the walls of hollow abdominal organs. Which muscle type is involved?
examQuestions.explanation: Smooth (visceral) muscle is composed of fusiform myocytes with single central nuclei and NO striations. It forms walls of hollow organs (GI tract, bladder, blood vessels). It is involuntary. Skeletal = multinucleate, peripheral nuclei, striated. Cardiac = single central nucleus, striated, intercalated discs.

examQuestions.question: Epithelial tissue is highly vascularized because it has numerous blood vessels running between the cells.
examQuestions.explanation: False. Epithelial tissue is AVASCULAR — it has NO blood vessels. Nutrients diffuse from the underlying connective tissue through the basement membrane. This is an important distinguishing feature: connective tissue is highly vascularized (except tendons and cartilage), while epithelium relies entirely on diffusion.

examQuestions.question: Stratified epithelia are named based on the shape of cells in the deepest (basal) layer.
examQuestions.explanation: False. Stratified epithelia are named based on the shape of the cells in the SURFACE (apical) layer, not the basal layer. For example, stratified squamous epithelium has flat surface cells even though basal cells are more cuboidal/columnar.

examQuestions.question: The first collagen deposited during wound healing is Type I, which is later replaced by stronger Type III collagen.
examQuestions.explanation: False. This is reversed. Type III collagen (reticular, 'bloody') is the FIRST collagen deposited during wound healing as granulation tissue forms. It is later remodeled and replaced by stronger Type I collagen during late wound repair (scar maturation), which provides greater tensile strength.

examQuestions.question: The pancreas is an example of a gland with both endocrine and exocrine functions.
examQuestions.explanation: True. The pancreas is a compound acinar gland with BOTH functions. Its exocrine function (acinar cells) secretes digestive enzymes through the pancreatic duct into the duodenum. Its endocrine function (islets of Langerhans) secretes insulin and glucagon directly into the bloodstream.

examQuestions.question: Fibrocartilage is the only cartilage type found in articular surfaces of synovial joints.
examQuestions.explanation: False. HYALINE cartilage lines articular surfaces of synovial joints (articular cartilage). Fibrocartilage is found at the pubic symphysis, menisci, and intervertebral discs — fibrocartilaginous joints, not synovial joints. Articular hyaline cartilage notably lacks perichondrium.

examQuestions.question: A histology student sees a tissue with abundant matrix, few cells dispersed throughout, no free surfaces, and visible blood vessels. Which tissue is most consistent with this description?
examQuestions.explanation: Connective tissue is characterized by: (1) cells do NOT cover or line surfaces (no free surfaces), (2) abundant extracellular matrix, (3) cells dispersed in ECM, (4) highly vascularized. Epithelial tissue has no ECM and is avascular. Muscle and nervous tissues have more cells relative to matrix.

examQuestions.question: Stereocilia in the epididymis are best characterized as:
options: Non-motile structures in the male reproductive tract
Modified cilia that create fluid currents
examQuestions.explanation: Stereocilia are NON-MOTILE elongated microvilli found in the epididymis (male reproductive tract) and inner ear hair cells. Despite the name, they are NOT true cilia (cilia have a 9+2 axoneme structure and are motile). They increase surface area for absorption of fluid from the seminiferous tubules and facilitate sperm maturation.

examQuestions.question: On H&E staining, collagen fibers appear pink (acidophilic). A special silver stain is required to visualize which fiber type?
examQuestions.explanation: Reticular fibers (Type III collagen) are argyrophilic — they selectively absorb silver stains, appearing dark brown/black. H&E stains make collagen appear pink. Elastic fibers can be stained with special elastic stains (e.g., Verhoeff-Van Gieson). Ground substance is unstained in routine H&E.

examQuestions.question: A 3-year-old child is brought to the ER with multiple unexplained fractures in various stages of healing. The pediatrician notes blue sclerae. Child abuse must be ruled out. What genetic diagnosis is also on the differential?
examQuestions.explanation: Osteogenesis Imperfecta (Type I collagen defect) causes fragile bones that fracture with minimal trauma. Blue sclerae result from thin sclerae allowing the underlying choroid to show through. OI can mimic child abuse, making it critical to consider when evaluating unexplained fractures in children.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following accurately describes the lamina propria?
options: The deep connective tissue layer of serous membranes
The areolar connective tissue underlying mucous membranes
The reticular fiber framework of lymphoid organs
The fibrous outer layer of cartilage
examQuestions.explanation: The lamina propria is the areolar connective tissue layer that directly underlies mucous membrane epithelium. It provides vascular support (nutrients/immune cells) to the avascular epithelium above. It is distinct from the submucosa, which lies deeper.

examQuestions.question: Which junction type is responsible for preventing passage of luminal gut bacteria into the bloodstream via the paracellular route?
examQuestions.explanation: Tight junctions (Zonula Occludens) at the apical end of enterocytes seal the paracellular space, preventing bacteria and antigens from passing between cells. This is the critical 'barrier' function of intestinal epithelium. Disruption leads to 'leaky gut' and may contribute to inflammatory conditions.

examQuestions.question: A footballer sustains a medial meniscus tear. The meniscus is composed of which cartilage type, and why does it heal poorly?
options: Elastic cartilage; abundant elastic fibers prevent repair
examQuestions.explanation: The menisci are fibrocartilage — large coarse collagen bundles that resist compression and absorb shock. Like all cartilage, fibrocartilage is avascular and aneural, severely limiting its healing capacity. This is why meniscal tears often require surgical intervention.

examQuestions.question: Cardiac muscle is classified as striated AND involuntary.
examQuestions.explanation: True. Cardiac muscle is the unique combination of striated AND involuntary. Skeletal muscle = striated + voluntary. Smooth muscle = non-striated + involuntary. Only cardiac muscle is both striated (organized sarcomeres) and involuntary (controlled by the SA node and autonomic nervous system).

examQuestions.question: A tissue biopsy shows a single layer of tall, narrow cells with oval nuclei in the basal half of each cell, scattered goblet cells, and a prominent brush border. Which epithelial type is this?
examQuestions.explanation: Simple columnar epithelium features: single row of tall narrow cells, oval nuclei in the basal half, a brush border (microvilli), goblet cells (mucus secretion), and may have cilia. Found in the GI tract, uterus, uterine tubes, and kidney collecting ducts. Pseudostratified appears multilayered but all cells touch the basement membrane.

examQuestions.question: Dense connective tissue contains fewer cells than loose connective tissue but has more abundant and closely packed fibers.
examQuestions.explanation: True. Dense connective tissue is characterized by more numerous, thicker, and denser (more closely packed) fibers with FEWER cells than loose connective tissue. The increased fiber density gives it greater tensile strength (dense regular for tendons) or multidirectional strength (dense irregular for dermis).

examQuestions.question: Adherens junctions (Zonula Adherens) circle the entire epithelial cell and form an adhesion belt. Which two structural proteins are linked at this junction?
examQuestions.explanation: The adherens junction connects actin filaments from the cytoskeleton to cadherins (transmembrane adhesion proteins). This creates a circumferential adhesion belt that resists cell separation during contractile activities (e.g., intestinal peristalsis). Loss of cadherins promotes metastasis.

examQuestions.question: Hyaline cartilage has a clear, glassy appearance because of the fineness of its ________ fibers, which are not visible by light microscopy.
examQuestions.explanation: Hyaline cartilage contains Type II collagen fibers that are so fine they are not individually visible with standard light microscopy, giving the matrix a clear, homogeneous, 'glassy' (hyaline = Greek for glass) appearance. The collagen is still present — it just cannot be resolved with standard staining.

examQuestions.question: The first connective tissue to form in embryos is called ________, derived from the mesoderm, and consists of embryonic stem cells in a gel matrix with wispy collagen fibers.
examQuestions.explanation: Mesenchyme is the embryonic stem cell-derived connective tissue (from mesoderm) that gives rise to all adult connective tissues. It consists of stellate cells in a gelatinous matrix with wispy collagen fibers and fibroblasts. It is not present in adults.

examQuestions.question: The process by which dead surface cells of stratified squamous epithelium flake off is called ________ or desquamation.
examQuestions.explanation: As cells in the deepest layers divide and migrate upward, surface cells eventually die and flake off — a process called exfoliation or desquamation. This is why Pap smears work (exfoliated cervical epithelial cells), and why forensic sampling from surfaces can recover epithelial cells.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the composition of the extracellular matrix (ECM) in connective tissue?
examQuestions.explanation: The ECM of connective tissue is composed of: (1) fibrous proteins (collagen, elastic, reticular fibers) and (2) ground substance (clear, colorless, viscous gel = tissue fluid). Cells are not part of the ECM — they reside within or between the ECM.

examQuestions.question: Which of the following correctly describes the mesothelium?
options: Stratified squamous epithelium lining body cavities
Simple squamous epithelium forming the epithelial layer of serous membranes
Simple cuboidal epithelium lining glandular ducts
Pseudostratified epithelium of the respiratory tract
examQuestions.explanation: The mesothelium is simple squamous epithelium that forms the surface layer of serous membranes (pleura, peritoneum, pericardium). It secretes serous fluid that reduces friction between the parietal and visceral layers during organ movement.

examQuestions.question: A patient is described as having anaplastic carcinoma. What does anaplasia imply about the tumor?
options: The tumor cells are well-differentiated and easily identified
The tumor has fewer cells than normal (hypoplasia)
The cells are poorly differentiated; degree of anaplasia correlates with malignancy
The tumor shows orderly growth with normal cellular architecture
examQuestions.explanation: Anaplasia = loss of differentiation. Anaplastic tumor cells are poorly differentiated — they no longer resemble their tissue of origin. The degree of anaplasia correlates with malignancy; more anaplastic = higher grade = more aggressive cancer. Pleomorphism (variation in cell/nuclear size and shape) is a hallmark feature.

examQuestions.question: Marfan's syndrome results from a defect in fibrillin-2 (FBN2) located on chromosome 5.
examQuestions.explanation: False. Marfan's syndrome is caused by a defect in fibrillin-1 (FBN1) located on chromosome 15. Fibrillin-2 (FBN2) mutations cause congenital contractural arachnodactyly (Beals syndrome), a different condition. The FBN1 defect leads to abnormal elastic tissue, causing the cardiovascular, skeletal, and ocular features of Marfan's.

examQuestions.question: What structural feature is responsible for compact bone's resistance to axial compression while maintaining some ability to tolerate bending forces?
options: Concentric lamellae of osteons around central canals
Abundance of reticular fibers in the bone matrix
High proportion of elastic fibers in the periosteum
examQuestions.explanation: The osteon system (Haversian system) of compact bone — concentric lamellae arranged in cylindrical units around central canals — provides mechanical strength. The alternating fiber orientations in successive lamellae distribute stress, resisting compression and torsional forces.

examQuestions.question: Which cell junction type is described as a 'basal specialization' that anchors epithelial cells to the basement membrane using integrin transmembrane proteins?
examQuestions.explanation: Hemidesmosomes are basal specializations that anchor basal epithelial cells to the underlying basement membrane. They use integrins (not cadherins) as their transmembrane glycoproteins and connect keratin intermediate filaments intracellularly to the extracellular basement membrane. Desmosomes connect adjacent cells laterally.
